title: [Bug]: Configuration JSON accepts non-standard constants
summary: The core configuration loader rejects duplicate JSON keys but still allows Python-specific constants such as NaN and Infinity. Those constants are not valid interoperable JSON and should fail before Pydantic validation or environment override handling.
observed: A configuration document containing an unused value such as NaN can be parsed by the low-level JSON loader instead of being rejected as invalid JSON at the trust boundary.
expected: The configuration loader should reject non-standard JSON constants consistently with payload, metrics, and NATS monitoring parsers.
reproduction: Run the focused regression test that writes a small configuration JSON document containing NaN and calls the core load_json helper.
impact: Configuration files are operator-controlled trust-boundary inputs. Accepting non-standard JSON makes behavior less portable and can hide malformed local configuration in public evidence or release checks.
delivery_semantics: The fix must not change commit-then-ACK behavior. It only tightens configuration parsing before the runner starts.
security: The fix must reject malformed configuration without printing secrets, environment values, payloads, or local deployment details.
acceptance: A focused regression test proves non-standard JSON constants are rejected by the configuration loader.
The loader error is human-readable and does not include sensitive values.
Valid JSON configuration files continue to load normally.
Existing unit, smoke, documentation, package, and release checks still pass.
tests: Run the focused regression test, configuration tests, CLI validation tests, and the full local check script.
documentation: Update CHANGELOG.md and security documentation to state that configuration parsing uses standards-compliant JSON only.
closeout: Close only after the release containing the fix is published. Include the release tag, regression evidence, verification evidence, and any remaining limitations.
